source.title: NADPH biosensor-based identification of an alcohol dehydrogenase variant with improved catalytic properties caused by a single charge reversal at the protein surface

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aimed to improve the catalytic properties of the NADPH-dependent alcohol dehydrogenase from Lactobacillus brevis (LbADH) for the reduction of 2,5-hexanedione to (2R,5R)-hexanediol, a valuable chiral building block. Existing high-throughput screening methods for ADH variants were limited in throughput (10^4-10^5 variants). The researchers hypothesized that a previously developed NADPH biosensor (pSenSox) coupled with fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) could enable screening of much larger libraries (10^7-10^9 variants) to identify beneficial mutations, even those at unexpected positions.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: A high-throughput screening approach using the NADPH biosensor pSenSox and FACS was applied to a random mutagenesis library of LbADH (~1.4×10^6 clones) to find variants with improved activity for 2,5-hexanedione reduction. The variant LbADH^K71E was identified, showing 16% lower KM and 17% higher Vmax for 2,5-hexanedione compared to wild-type. Improved activity was also observed for several other substrates. K71 is solvent-exposed and distant from the active site, making it a non-obvious target for rational engineering. The study demonstrates the power of the biosensor-based screening method.
process_signals: Identification of a single beneficial mutation (K71E) from a large random library
Improved kinetic parameters for the target substrate and broader substrate range
Mutation located at a surface position not previously considered for engineering
metrics: Improved activity for acetophenone, acetylpyridine, 2-hexanone, 4-hydroxy-2-butanone, methyl acetoacetate

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: LbADH is a robust, stereoselective NADPH-dependent ADH used for chiral alcohol synthesis. Its efficiency is influenced by substrate properties. Directed evolution is limited by screening throughput; typical assays handle 10^4-10^5 variants. FACS can screen 10^7-10^9 variants if a suitable assay exists. The pSenSox biosensor, based on the SoxR transcription factor and eYFP reporter, responds to NADPH consumption and was previously shown to correlate with LbADH activity. A proof-of-concept study identified an LbADH variant with slightly increased activity but reduced affinity for 4-methyl-2-pentanone. The present study aimed to apply this system to screen for improved 2,5-hexanedione reduction.
process_signals: Prior work: biosensor validated for LbADH activity, but previous variant had trade-off (increased activity, reduced affinity)
Goal: find variants with improved catalytic properties for a specific substrate
failures_or_limits: Previous proof-of-concept variant had reduced substrate affinity, indicating a trade-off

section_gists.heading: Materials and methods
section_gists.gist: This section contains detailed experimental procedures but the provided text only includes the heading.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: This section contains the results but the provided text only includes the heading.

section_gists.heading: Construction of an LbADH library and FACS screening
section_gists.gist: A library of LbADH variants was generated by error-prone PCR, yielding ~1.4×10^6 clones. The library was expressed in E. coli with pSenSox. Cells were incubated with 70 mM 2,5-hexanedione for 2.5 h (optimal time determined from preliminary studies). FACS screening involved three positive selection steps for high fluorescence, one negative selection to exclude constitutively fluorescent cells, and a fourth positive selection. Out of 2.5×10^5 analyzed cells, 240 high-fluorescence cells were sorted; 83 formed colonies.
process_signals: Screening strategy: multiple rounds of positive and negative selection to reduce false positives
Only 35% of sorted cells formed colonies, indicating some loss during sorting or non-viable cells
failures_or_limits: 65% of sorted cells did not form colonies, possibly due to sorting stress or false positives

section_gists.heading: Initial characterization of isolated clones in the BioLector
section_gists.gist: The 83 clones were cultivated in a BioLector with/without 2,5-hexanedione to confirm fluorescence. Several showed increased specific fluorescence compared to wild-type. Sequencing of four clones revealed all carried the same K71E mutation (G→A transition). The repeated isolation of the same mutation was attributed to the stringent selection procedure. The mutation was re-cloned into fresh pSenSox and confirmed to confer higher fluorescence, ruling out secondary mutations.
process_signals: Validation step: BioLector cultivation confirmed increased fluorescence for several clones
Sequencing of four clones all showed K71E, suggesting strong selection pressure
Re-cloning confirmed the mutation alone was responsible
methods: BioLector cultivation with online fluorescence and biomass monitoring
Re-cloning of LbADH^K71E into fresh pSenSox
failures_or_limits: Only four clones sequenced; other clones may have had different mutations but were not pursued
The selection procedure may have been too stringent, reducing diversity

section_gists.heading: Purification and biochemical characterization of LbADH^WT and LbADH^K71E
section_gists.gist: Both enzymes were purified via StrepTag-II affinity and size-exclusion chromatography. LbADH^K71E eluted slightly earlier in SEC, suggesting a structural change (apparent mass 113 kDa vs 95 kDa for WT). SDS-PAGE showed a slightly higher apparent mass for the variant. DLS indicated slightly smaller radius for variant but not significant. Thermal shift assay showed variant melting point 52.8°C vs 51.5°C for WT, indicating slightly higher stability. Kinetic analysis: for 2,5-hexanedione, K71E had KM 4.3 mM (16% lower) and Vmax 173.3 μmol/min/mg (17% higher) than WT. For methyl acetoacetate, K71E also showed better affinity and higher Vmax. Activity assays with four other substrates showed 21-39% higher activity for K71E. No activity with NADH.
process_signals: Structural differences observed: SEC elution shift, SDS-PAGE shift, slight stability increase
Kinetic improvements confirmed for target substrate and extended to other substrates
failures_or_limits: Only two substrates fully characterized kinetically; others tested at single concentration

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: The K71E mutation is on the protein surface, ~28 Å from substrate and ~14 Å from NADPH, not directly involved in catalysis. The charge reversal (Lys+ to Glu-) may alter surface electrostatics, potentially affecting substrate access or dynamics. The study demonstrates that FACS-based screening with a biosensor can identify non-obvious beneficial mutations. Limitations: the mechanism of improvement is not fully elucidated; only one variant was characterized in depth; the library may not have been fully explored due to stringent selection. Future work could investigate the structural basis and apply the method to other enzymes.
process_signals: Mutation is distant from active site, highlighting the power of unbiased screening
Charge reversal may affect protein dynamics or substrate channeling
Acknowledgment that the exact mechanism remains speculative
methods: Structural analysis using PyMOL and crystal structure (PDB not specified in text)
failures_or_limits: Mechanism of improvement not experimentally determined
Only one variant (K71E) emerged from screening, possibly due to stringent selection
Library diversity may not have been fully sampled
No in vivo biotransformation performance data beyond fluorescence

section_gists.heading: Availability of data and materials
section_gists.gist: Strains and plasmids are available from the corresponding author upon request.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The researchers hypothesized that the NADPH biosensor pSenSox could be used with FACS to screen large random mutagenesis libraries of LbADH for improved activity toward 2,5-hexanedione, overcoming throughput limitations of conventional assays.
They expected to find beneficial mutations, possibly at unexpected positions, due to the unbiased nature of the screen.

The identification of only the K71E variant after stringent selection suggests that this mutation was strongly enriched, possibly because it provided a significant fitness advantage in the screening conditions.

The improved activity for multiple substrates suggests that the K71E mutation may generally enhance enzyme function, perhaps by altering protein dynamics or substrate access rather than directly affecting the active site.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What is the molecular mechanism by which the K71E surface mutation improves catalytic activity?
Were there other beneficial mutations in the library that were lost due to the stringent selection or low colony recovery?
How does the K71E variant perform in preparative biotransformations (e.g., yield, enantioselectivity, stability under process conditions)?
Can this screening approach be generalized to other NADPH-dependent enzymes and substrates?
What is the structural basis for the observed shift in SEC elution and SDS-PAGE mobility?

events.summary: FACS screening involved three positive selections, one negative selection, and a final positive selection.
240 high-fluorescence cells were sorted; 83 formed colonies for further analysis.

events.summary: FACS screening strategy designed with multiple positive and negative selection steps to enrich LbADH variants with improved 2,5-hexanedione reduction.
FACS screening of LbADH library: 2.5×10^5 cells analyzed, 240 high-fluorescence cells sorted, 83 formed colonies.
BioLector cultivation confirmed increased specific fluorescence for several clones in presence of 2,5-hexanedione.
Sequencing of four clones revealed all carried the same K71E mutation (G→A transition).

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers FACS screening, clone characterization, purification, and kinetic analysis, providing key experimental events.

events.summary: No activity with NADH, confirming cofactor specificity unchanged.
Structural analysis shows K71 is solvent-exposed and distant from active site.
The mechanism of improvement by K71E is not fully elucidated.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed experimental results (thermal shift, kinetics, substrate profiling) and structural analysis of the K71E variant, directly supporting the paper's core findings.

events.summary: Structural analysis of LbADH^WT and LbADH^K71E using PyMOL and crystal structure PDB 1ZK4 to visualize the mutation location.
Observation that LbADH^K71E shows slower migration on SDS-PAGE, attributed to lower pI causing stronger negative charge repulsion with SDS.
Hypothesis that the K71E mutation might stabilize the charged transition state during catalysis, enhancing enzyme activity.
Conclusion that pSenSox is suitable for FACS-based high-throughput screening of ADH libraries to identify improved variants.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains structural analysis, mechanistic hypotheses, computational stability predictions, experimental validation, and a concluding validation of the screening method.
This chunk contains only reference list and publisher information, with no research process events.

evidence_bank.quote: The strategy used to isolate cells containing LbADH variants with improved activity for reduction of 2,5-hexanedione included three enrichments steps with positive selection followed by one negative selection step and another positive selection step.
240 library cells showing a higher fluorescence than the reference were spotted on LB agar plates
83 out of the 240 spotted cells formed colonies
The entire screening process comprised three positive selection steps for cells showing high fluorescence after biotransformation of 2,5-hexanedione followed by a negative selection step.

In the latter, cells showing high fluorescence independent of 2,5-hexanedione were excluded by collecting only those cells in the library that showed the same fluorescence as E. coli TOP10/pSenNeg cells lacking an active LbADH.
83 cells (35%) formed colonies and were further analyzed.

Several of the clones showed an increased specific fluorescence in the presence of 2,5-hexanedione compared to the absence of 2,5-hexanedione and a higher specific fluorescence than the reference strain with wild-type LbADH.
Sequencing of the plasmids isolated from four of these clones revealed that all carried a single G→A transition in the Lbadh gene resulting in the amino acid exchange K71E.
No activity was found when NADPH was replaced by NADH.
position 71 is not located close to the active center at the protein core, but solvent-exposed on the protein surface
the distance of position 71 to the substrate acetophenone is around 28.0 Å and to the cofactor NADPH approximately 14.1 Å
the amino acid at position 71 is not directly involved in substrate binding and reduction
The images were generated with the software PyMOL 2.2.0 (PDB code 1ZK4)

The K71E exchange might have a positive influence on enzyme catalysis by stabilizing the charged transition state.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Improve catalytic properties of LbADH for 2,5-hexanedione reduction using NADPH biosensor-based FACS screening to overcome throughput limits.

canonical_events.summary: Design FACS screening strategy with multiple positive/negative selections to enrich improved variants.
Generate random mutagenesis library of LbADH (~1.4×10^6 clones) by error-prone PCR.
FACS screening: 2.5×10^5 cells analyzed, 240 high-fluorescence cells sorted, 83 colonies recovered (35% recovery).
BioLector cultivation confirms increased specific fluorescence for several clones vs. wild-type.
Sequencing of four clones reveals all carry K71E mutation (G→A transition).
Re-cloning K71E into fresh pSenSox confirms mutation alone causes increased fluorescence.
Purify LbADH^WT and LbADH^K71E; SEC shows apparent mass shift (95 vs 113 kDa), SDS-PAGE shift, DLS no significant difference.
Thermal shift assay: K71E melting point 52.8°C vs WT 51.5°C, indicating slightly higher stability.
Kinetic analysis: for 2,5-hexanedione, K71E KM 4.3 mM (16% lower), Vmax 173.3 μmol/min/mg (17% higher) vs WT.
K71E shows improved activity for other substrates (21-39% higher) and better kinetics for methyl acetoacetate.
Structural analysis: K71 is solvent-exposed, ~28 Å from substrate, ~14 Å from NADPH; mutation distant from active site.
Mechanism of K71E improvement not fully elucidated; only one variant characterized; stringent selection may have limited diversity.

edges.reason: Sorted clones are validated in BioLector.
Mutation is re-cloned to confirm causality.
Confirmed variant is purified for characterization.
Purified protein used for stability assay.
Purified protein used for kinetic analysis.
Kinetic improvement prompts testing on other substrates.
Structural distance highlights limitation in mechanistic understanding.

must_cover_points: Problem: limited throughput of conventional ADH screening.
Hypothesis: pSenSox biosensor with FACS enables high-throughput screening.
Library generation: ~1.4×10^6 clones by error-prone PCR.
Mutation identification: K71E found in all sequenced clones.
Re-cloning confirms K71E alone causes phenotype.
Purification and biophysical characterization: SEC shift, SDS-PAGE shift, slight stability increase.
Kinetic improvement: KM 16% lower, Vmax 17% higher for 2,5-hexanedione.
Broadened substrate scope: 21-39% higher activity for other substrates.
Structural analysis: K71 is surface-exposed, distant from active site.
Limitation: mechanism not fully understood; only one variant characterized.
